coredata.dc:title: CaIn2S4 decorated WS2 hybrid for efficient Cr(VI) reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Baibai
Liu, Xinjuan
Li, Lei
Zhuge, Zhihao
Li, Yiqi
Li, Can
Gong, Yinyan
Niu, Lengyuan
Xu, Shiqing
Sun, Chang Q.
coredata.dc:description: Exploiting efficient visible light active photocatalyst is a great challenge with potential applications such as environmental pollution and solar energy conversion. Herein, a series of CaIn2S4/WS2 hybrids was fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. WS2 as cocatalyst increases the visible light absorption and separation efficient of charge carrier at the interface, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CaIn2S4/WS2. CaIn2S4/20 wt% WS2 hybrid shows the highest Cr(VI) reduction rate of 98% and methyl orange degradation rate of 96%. This work could offer some thought for designing and fabricating hybrid photocatalyst with high efficient and stable activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible light active photocatalyst
CaIn2S4
WS2
Co-catalyst
Cr(VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of WS2, CIS and CW-20.
FESEM images (a) of pure CIS; (b) CW-20, (c) low- and (d) high-resolution TEM images, (e) SAED pattern of CW-20.
XPS spectra of (a) Ca 2p, (b) In 3d, (c) S 2p and (d) W 4f for CW-20.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30.
(a) Cr(VI) reduction rates after 90 min visible light irradiation over pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30; (b–c) catalytic reaction kinetics of Cr(VI) with time; (d) photostability of CW-20.
(a) Nyquist plots and (b) time resolved PL spectra of pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30.

(a) Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by CW-20 with the addition of electron scavenger; (b) photocatalytic MO degradation by CW-20 with the addition of h+, OH and O2
− scavengers; (c) EPR spectra of DMPO-O2
− in the presence of CW-20 and MO under dark and visible light irradiation; (d) XPS spectrum of Cr 2p for CW-20 after the catalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: CaIn2S4 decorated WS2 hybrid for efficient Cr(VI) reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Coordination Bond Metrology and Engineering, College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Coordination Bond Metrology and Engineering
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Extraordinary Coordination Bond and Advanced Materials Techniques, Yangtze Normal University, Chongqing 408100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Extraordinary Coordination Bond and Advanced Materials Techniques

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploiting efficient visible light active photocatalyst is a great challenge with potential applications such as environmental pollution and solar energy conversion. Herein, a series of CaIn2S4/WS2 hybrids was fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. WS2 as cocatalyst increases the visible light absorption and separation efficient of charge carrier at the interface, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CaIn2S4/WS2. CaIn2S4/20 wt% WS2 hybrid shows the highest Cr(VI) reduction rate of 98% and methyl orange degradation rate of 96%. This work could offer some thought for designing and fabricating hybrid photocatalyst with high efficient and stable activity.

CaIn2S4/WS2 hybrids display excellent photocatalytic activity for Cr(VI) reduction and MO degradation under visible light irradiation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Due to global warming and uncontrolled use resources, environmental contaminate and energy shortage have become very severe issue in the world [1–6]. Hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) is highly toxic to marine organisms, and can cause the cancer, perforation of nasal septum and kidney damage to human beings, which widely used in the fields of electroplating, leather tanning, metal finishing, textile manufacturing, steel fabricating, paint and pigments, fertilizing, and so on [7]. The World Health Organization recommended that the Cr(VI) concentration in drinking water cannot exceed 0.003 mg/L. The Environmental Protection Agency stipulated that the Cr(VI) concentration should be <0.005 mg/L. Various techniques such as adsorption, ultrafiltration, reverse osmosis, and coagulation have been used to remove the Cr(VI). However, they are complicated, high power consumption and high cost. Developing an alternative and renewable energy sources is an urgent global need for environmental and economic reason.

Reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is considered as an efficient strategy, because less toxic Cr(III) is readily precipitated to form the Cr2O3 or Cr(OH)3 in alkaline solution [8]. Photocatalysis has attracted considerable focus as a sustainable approach for solving the environmental and energy problem. Potential photocatalysts such as TiO2 and ZnO exhibit excellent ultraviolet light catalytic activity due to their outstanding physical and chemistry capability [9–11]. To make full use of solar energy, developing highly photocatalysts with visible light active is very importance for actual application. To date, various oxides, sulfides, and nitrides based photocatalysts have been proved to show considerable catalytic activity for water splitting, reduction of CO2 and heavy metal ion, and degradation of organic pollution [12–16]. Though great progress has been made, the higher catalytic activity is still desirable because of the low charge carriers separation efficiency, limiting their practical application.

Metal sulfide photocatalysts with narrow band gap are promising candidates owing to excellent catalytic activity [17–20]. Nevertheless, they are toxicity and unstable during the photocatalytic reaction process. Ternary metal sulfides have also aroused great interest in the photocatalysis filed because of the unique chemical and photoelectronic property [21]. In particular, CaIn2S4 (CIS) with band gap of ~1.9 eV has been regarded as an effective photocatalyst with visible light active owing to the good catalytic activity and high stability [22]. Ding et al. [23] synthesized CIS via a facile hydrothermal method followed calcination process, and found that CIS showed the maximum H2 production rate with 30.92 μmol g−1 h−1. Furthermore, monoclinic CIS was also fabricated via a high temperature sulfurization approach and the H2 production rate reached to 30.2 μmol h−1 [24]. Nonetheless, their application in photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction systems has yet to be exploited.

The fast charge recombination causes the low catalytic activity of pure ternary metal sulfide photocatalysts. Intensive efforts have been made to increase their photocatalytic performances, such as constructing nanostructure and heterojunction, and surface modification with cocatalyst [25,26]. The surface modification with cocatalyst has been demonstrated to be a valid mean to promote the transfer efficient and suppress the charge recombination at the interface of composites, thereby greatly enhancing the catalytic activity [27]. g-C3N4, reduced graphene oxide, and alloy (AuCu and AuPt) as cocatalysts have been reported to separate the charge carries and improve their catalytic activity in the H2 production and degradation of pharmaceutical compounds [28,29]. Recently, much research attentions have been paid on the transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) owing to the unique two dimensional structure, and good physical and chemical property for wide applications in the lubricants, batteries, supercapacitors, and photocatalysis [30–33]. Previously, we found MoSe2 and MoSe2-based photocatalysts exhibit outstanding absorption and good photocatalytic activity under whole solar spectrum irradiation [34–36]. Besides MoSe2, as one of the typical 2D TMDs, WS2 has a direct band gap and emits fluorescence, which allows it to be applied in photocatalysis and optoelectronic devices field [37–39]. So far, WS2 as cocatalysts enhance the H2 production rate of CdS [40], MoS2 [41], carbon nitride [42] owing to their synergistic effect. Therefore, it is logical to trust that the introduction of WS2 into CIS to form CIS/WS2 (CW) hybrid is a valid mean to improve the catalytic activity of CIS, which has not been studied yet.

In this study, we fabricated the CW hybrids via a facile hydrothermal method. The visible light catalytic Cr(VI) reduction activity was investigated. The introduction of WS2 as cocatalysts can increase the absorption and charge separation efficient due to their synergy effect between CIS and WS2. Compared with pure CIS, CW hybrid shows higher visible light catalytic activity under visible light irradiation.

Fig. 1 shows the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of CIS, WS2 and CW-20. For pure CIS, all diffraction peaks correspond to the cubic phase of CIS with space group Fd3m (JCPDS 31-0272) [23]. The XRD diffraction peaks of WS2 can be indexed to the hexagonal WS2 (JCPDS 08-0237) [43]. The XRD pattern of CW hybrid is similar to that of pure CIS, confirming that the composite of WS2 cannot change the crystal structure of CIS during the preparation process. The new peaks corresponding to the WS2 are observed in XRD pattern of CW-20 compared with pure CIS. Furthermore, no other impurity peaks is found, which proves the pure phase of CW hybrid. All results demonstrate the existence of CIS and WS2 in the CW hybrids.

The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images of CIS and CW-20 are shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b). CIS is composed of by nano-sized particles. When the WS2 sheets are introduced into pure CIS, the CIS particles are well dispersed on surrounding of WS2 sheets, forming a good interface between them. The transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CW-20 are shown in Fig. 2(c–d). CIS particles are embedded into the WS2 sheets. The intimate interface contact between CIS and WS2 is rewarding to the charge carries transport, thus improving the photocatalytic activity. The lattice spacing is calculated as 0.31 nm and 0.63, which can be assigned to the (222) crystal plane of cubic CIS and (002) crystal plane of hexagonal WS2, respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2(e) has the clear diffraction rings, demonstrating a high crystallinity of CW hybrid.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was employed to demonstrate the elemental composition, chemical environment and oxidation states of CW hybrid, as shown in Fig. 3 . The Ca 2p in Fig. 3(a) splits into two peaks at 348.5 and 352.1 eV, corresponding to the Ca 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively [44,45]. From Fig. 3(b), the In 3d can be fitted into two peaks located at 445.4 and 452.9 eV, which are attributed to the In 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 with a peak splitting of around 7.5 eV [46,47]. High resolution S 2p spectra in Fig. 3(c) shows two characteristic peaks at 161.7 and 162.8 eV corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks, respectively, which suggests the presence of S2− species [22]. The W 4f signal can be splited into two peaks located at 32.5 and 34.7 eV, which are ascribed to the W 4f7/2 and 4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 3(d)) [43]. All results further indicate that CW hybrids are synthesized though a hydrothermal reaction process.

Fig. 4(a) shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30. Pure CIS has good absorption with the absorption edge at about 684 nm. With the increase of WS2 amount, the absorption intensity of CW hybrid increases, which is mainly caused by the background absorption of WS2 [48]. This is advantageous for the catalytic reaction process. Furthermore, the band gaps of CIS, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30 are 1.68, 1.58, 1.45 and 1.34 eV, respectively, demonstrating that the introduction of WS2 cannot affect the inherent band structure of pure CIS, but increased the visible light absorption [45,49,50]. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30 are shown in Fig. 4(b). As shown, all samples show type IV isotherms with a hysteresis loop of H3, confirming the presence of mesoporous structure. The BET surface area, pore volume and average pore size of pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30 are summarized in Table S1. As shown, the BET surface area and pore volume decrease with the increase of WS2 content owing to the low BET surface area of WS2, which is not in accordance with catalytic activity. Therefore, the BET surface area and pore volume have no obvious effect on the photocatalytic reaction process [34].

The visible light photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction activity of pure CIS, WS2, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30 was performed (Fig. 5(a)). Blank experiment was also performed without photocatalyst under the same experimental conditions for comparison. As shown, the concentration of Cr(VI) has little change in blank experiment, which indicates that the Cr(VI) is reduced in the visible light catalytic process. The Cr(VI) reduction rate of pure CIS is 78% after 90 min irradiation. When the WS2 is introduction into pure CIS, the catalytic activity of CW hybrids is sensitive to the WS2 doping amount. With the increase of WS2 amount, the photocatalytic activity first increases and then decreases. After 90 min irradiation, the Cr(VI) reduction rates are 85%, 98% and 73% for CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30, respectively. Therefore, the composite of WS2 affects noticeably the catalytic activity of CW hybrids. The optimal doping content is found to be 20 wt%. The values of rate constants (K) are 0.016, 0.020, 0.034 and 0.014 for pure CIS, CW-10, CW-20 and CW-30, respectively (Fig. 5(b–c)). CW-20 has the highest Cr(VI) reduction rate. Furthermore, photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) over CW-20 was also investigated (Fig. S1). The MO degradation rate reaches to 96% after 40 min irradiation. Therefore, the CW hybrid exhibits considerable catalytic activity in the heavy metal reduction and dye degradation.

To evaluate the photostability of photocatalysts for practical application, the cycling experiment was carried out (Fig. 5(d)). Results show that the Cr(VI) reduction rate has no obvious decrease after three cycling uses. Moreover, the crystal structure and chemical states of CW-20 after the catalytic reaction were characterized by XRD and XPS (Figs. S2 and S3). For XRD patterns and XPS spectra, no significant difference is found before and after the catalytic reaction, confirming that CW hybrid can be regarded as novel photocatalysts with visible light active.

The photocatalytic reaction process mainly includes the light absorption, redox reaction, and charge carrier transfer and separate. The charge carrier transfer and separate plays a decisive role in the catalytic reaction, which was investigated by the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) (Fig. 6(a)). The values of R s, R ct, CPE and Z W are fitted and listed in Table S2. The R ct first decreases and then increases with the increase of WS2 amount. Results confirm that the appropriate WS2 content can facilitate the transfer and prolong the charge carrier lifetime, consequently enhancing the catalytic activity. A similar phenomenon was also confirmed by the time resolved photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 6(b)). The values of the average charge carrier's lifetimes follow the order: 0.26 (CW-20) >0.24 (CW-10) >0.23 (CW-30) >0.18 (CIS) >0.16 (WS2). Time resolved PL spectra and EIS results demonstrate that CW-20 shows the maximum lifetime, demonstrating the efficient charge carrier's separation in the CW hybrid, in accordance with the result of photocatalytic experiment. Therefore, WS2 as cocatalyst in the CW hybrid can effectively inhibit the charge carrier recombination, consequently enhancing the catalytic activity. The excellent photocatalytic activity is ascribed to the good absorption in visible light region and the efficient charge separation. The WS2 content can affect the behaviors of separation and recombination of charge carries in the CW hybrid, and an appropriate WS2 content is important to the catalytic activity of CW hybrid [51]. However, the photocatalytic activity of CW-30 decreases, which should be ascribed to their increased recombination of charge carries and shielding effect [52].

The photoinduced electrons involve the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction process, while the hydroxyl radicals (OH), photoinduced holes (h+), and superoxide radical (O2
−) mainly take part in the photocatalytic degradation dye process. To prove the role of radicals, controlled trapping experiments have been performed with addition of different scavengers, such as AgNO3, KI, tertbutanol (t-BuOH) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) for electron, h+, OH and O2
− scavenger, respectively. The Cr(VI) reduction rate for CW-20 decreases obviously with the addition of AgNO3. Results indicate that the electrons determine the Cr(VI) reduction process (Fig. 7(a)). The addition of t-BuOH and KI affected slightly the MO degradation rate, while the MO degradation rate decreases largely by the addition of BQ, confirming that the O2
− determines the MO degradation process (Fig. 7(b)). To further verify the active radical in the photocatalytic degradation, the spin trapping electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was employed (Fig. 7(c)). There are no signals in the dark, while the characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− can be found under light irradiation. Therefore, O2
− is formed and is the chief active radical in the catalytic process, in agreement with the result of trapping experiment.

From above results and the band gap structures of CIS and WS2, an possible catalytic mechanism for CW hybrid is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The valence band (VB) electrons of CIS and WS2 are aroused under light irradiation to form the electron-hole pairs. The electrons at the conduction band (CB) of CIS can reduce the O2 to produce O2 −, because the O2 −/O2 potential (−0.28 V, vs. NHE [53]) is more positive than the CB of CIS (−1.1 V vs. NHE [53]). If the electrons transfer from the CB of CIS to WS2, the accumulated electrons on the CB of WS2 cannot reduce O2 into O2
− radicals, because the CB of WS2 (0.42 V vs. NHE [54]) is more positive than the redox potential of O2
− formation. Trapping experiments and EPR results manifest that the O2
− active radical plays an importance role in the catalytic MO degradation. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of WS2 are transferred to the VB of CIS, and the holes recombine in the VB of CIS [53]. The charge carrier transfer efficiency is enhanced and the charge carrier recombination is inhibited at the interface of CIS and WS2, consequently enhancing the catalytic nature.

In the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction process, the CB of CIS and WS2 is more negative than the potential of Cr(VI)/Cr(III) (0.51 V vs. NHE), the electrons can reduce the Cr(VI) to Cr(III), in agreement with the reported results [55]. The Cr(III) was proved by XPS spectrum of Cr 2p (Fig. 7(d)). The H2O may be oxidized to O2 by the photoinduced holes, as the redox potential for the same is more negative than the VB potential of WS2 [56]. The reaction steps are provided:
CIS /
WS 2
+ hν →
h +
+
e −
Cr 2
O 7
2 −
+ 14
H +
+ 6
e −
→ 2
Cr
3 +
+ 7
H 2
O
2
H 2
O + 4
h +
→
O 2
+ 4
H +

In photocatalytic MO degradation process, the electrons at the CB of CIS can reduce the O2 to produce O2 −. However, the OH/H2O potential (2.27 V, vs. NHE [53]) is more positive than the VB of CIS (+0.88 V vs. NHE [53]) and WS2 (1.81 V vs. NHE [54]), thus the OH active radical cannot be formed. Moreover, the holes also may be contributed to the catalytic degradation process. Trapping experiments and EPR results also demonstrate that the O2
− active radical plays an importance role in the catalytic MO degradation. The reaction steps are provided:
CIS /
WS 2
+ hν →
h +
+
e −
e −
+
O 2
→
O 2
· −
O 2
· −
+ MO →
H 2
O +
CO 2

In summary, a series of CW hybrid are successfully fabricated by the facile hydrothermal method for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of MO. CW hybrids show good visible light absorption. WS2 as cocatalyst can enhance the separate efficiency and lengthen the lifetime of charge carrier at the interface due to their synergy effect, greatly improving the catalytic activity. CW hybrids exhibit outstanding catalytic activity for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of MO compared with pure CIS. Highest Cr(VI) reduction and MO degradation rates of >96% are achieved for CW hybrid with 20 wt% WS2 under visible light irradiation. The optimal amount of WS2 is found to be 20 wt%. The excellent photocatalytic activity is ascribed to the good absorption in visible light region and the efficient charge separation. The photoinduced electrons and O2
− active radical dominant the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and MO degradation process, respectively. The work may provide some useful information about designing efficient visible light active photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the province Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LY18E060005, LY18E020007, LY19E020006 and LQ18E030005) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.03.322.
